TheReviewofFinancialStudies
---------------------------

Biased by Choice: How Financial Constraints Can Reduce Financial Mistakes
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

Rawley Z. Heimer

Boston College

AlexImas
--------

University of Chicago

We show that constraints can improve financial decision-making by disciplining behavioral biases. In financial markets, restrictions on leverage limit traders' ability to borrow to open new positions. We demonstrate that regulation that restricts the provision of leverage to retail traders improves trading performance.By increasing the opportunity cost of postponing the realization of losses, leverage constraints improve traders' market timing andreducetheirdispositioneffect.Wereplicatethesefindingsintwodistinctexperimental settings, further isolating the mechanism and demonstrating generality of the results. The interaction between constraints and behavioral biases has implications for policy and choice architecture.(JEL G40, G41, D18,D91,G11,G50)

Received January 10, 2019; editorial decision November 22, 2020 by Editor Lauren Cohen. Authors have furnished an Internet Appendix,which is available on the Oxford University PressWeb site next to the link to the final published paper online..

People often face constraints on their decision-making. In financial markets, investors are constrained by factors such as direct trading restrictions, illiquidity, and limits on leverage. According to standard theories of decision-making, people will be at least as well off, if not better, when such constraints are lifted. The logic is straightforward: any choice available when facing constraints also will be available when they are relaxed, in addition to other choices that may be even more attractive.

This paper offers a new behavioral perspective on how restricting choice may lead to better outcomes: constraints make the avoidance of psychologically painful decisions more costly, which can discipline observed behavioral biases. We provide evidence for this claim by examining the effects of borrowing constraints on investment decisions. Personal investing is the ideal venue to test how constraints interact with painful decisions because of the real, salient stakes involved and the well-documented tendency to delay realizing losses relative to gains (Shefrin and Statman 1985; Weber and Camerer 1998; Odean 1998). The phenomenon of investors holding on to losses has been shown to be suboptimal, leading to lower earnings (Shumway and Wu 2005). Such behavior has been termed the "disposition effect"' and has been documented across a wide range of markets and populations (for a review, see Kaustia, 2010).

We thank the editor (Lauren Cohen), two anonymous referees, the social network for providing us the data, Alex Dusenbery for helping with the database, and Christy Kang for research assistance. We thank discussants and participants at the following conferences: AFA (Justin Birru), Experimental Finance Conference, Helsinki Finance Summit (Samuli Knipfer), Miami Behavioral Finance Conference (Tom Chang), and SFS Cavalcade (Heather Tookes). We also thank seminar participants at Boston College, Cornell, and UBC. The paper was previously titled "Doing Less with More." Any remaining errors or omissions are our responsibility. Send correspondence to Rawley Heimer, [heimer@bc.edu](mailto:heimer@bc.edu)
.

One of the most parsimonious explanations for investors' unwillingness to realize losses stems from people's reluctance to make a psychologically painful decision. Barberis and Xiong (2012) argue that investors derive negative (positive) realization utility from selling an asset at a loss (gain). Within their framework, the disposition effect is generated in part by investors systematically putting off the painful realization of a loss in favor of realizing a gain. Moreover, investors may defer realizing losses until prompted by some exogenous event (e.g., a liquidity shock).

We show that leverage constraints can improve outcomes and performance by disciplining investors' behavioral biases, spurring them to realize losses sooner rather than later. The intuition is as follows. Suppose an investor wants to purchase asset X. However, the investor lacks the free cash flow to do so because she holds asset Y at a loss. An unconstrained investor can use leverage to purchase asset X and delay the psychological cost of selling asset Y. On the other hand, a constrained investor has to liquidate asset Y in order to make the purchase. In this way, constraints generate an opportunity cost for holding on to existing assets that can de-bias the investor's decisions. Relaxing the leverage constraint eliminates this opportunity cost, which leaves more room for psychological frictions to affect behavior.

We use multiple sources of data to provide evidence for this disciplining effect of leverage constraints. First, we examine the trading of retail investors in the market for foreign exchange (forex) before and after regulation that restricts their access to leverage. Second, we explore the effect of leverage on investment behavior in two incentivized laboratory experiments. The studies are complimentary: results from the forex market offer insight from a more naturalistic environment, while the experimental findings replicate mechanism.

To identify how leverage affects trading behavior in a natural setting, we exploit a 2010 CFTC regulation that restricts brokerages? provision of leverage to traders in the retail market for foreign exchange at a cap of 50:1 per trade. Prior to the regulation, these brokerages set leverage limits well above the 50:1 constraint. Crucial for identification, most brokerages have both U.S. and European clients trading the same assets. These European traders have similar characteristics (e.g., demographics and trading strategies) as their U.S. counterparts but are not subject to the regulation. Moreover, the trading performance of European and U.s. traders comoves prior to the regulation, making the former a suitable control group to study the effect of leverage on trading.

Using a data set that includes around 270,000 trades made by 1,000 traders within the 3 months that surround this legislation, we document a robust negative correlation between the amount of leverage used and traders' market timing per trade. We provide evidence of a causal relationship by estimating difference-in-differences regressions that compare U.s. traders subject to restrictions on leverage to unregulated European traders. The leverage constraint improves U.S. traders' performance by 1.9 basis points (bps) per trade, an increase of approximately one-tenth of the interquartile range of unlevered returns. Consistent with the results being caused by the leverage constraint, the effect nearly doubles when the constraint is binding—among traders who used at least 50:1 leverage prior to the regulation. Moreover, the absence of preexisting trends prior to the regulation further supports a causal relation.

realize losses. We find that traders who were most reluctant to realize losses relative to gains before the regulation—those with the highest disposition effect—experienced the largest improvements in trading performance after the regulation was imposed. Furthermore, we find that the reduction in available leverage directly reduces traders' disposition effect by about 13%. Consistent with our prediction, this decrease is driven primarily by a greater willingness to realize losses earlier; the regulation had no effect on the propensity to realize gains. Importantly, the decrease in the disposition effect is confined to traders' market orders and not their limit orders. Traders manually issue market orders to immediately execute. In contrast, traders set limit orders to close positions automatically at a predetermined price. In this way, limit orders act as a commitment device to trade at a prespecified gain or loss. The distinction between the ex ante and ex post decisions to realize a loss (limit orders versus market orders, respectively) provides evidence for leverage off realizing losses.1

To complement the findings from the forex market, we conducted two laboratory experiments. The first experiment was designed to mirror the forex setting while allowing for more extensive tests of the proposed mechanism, and to compare performance relative to a normative benchmark. Investors held shares of up to three assets. In each period, they could buy or sell shares, or both, depending on the treatment. Each asset's probability of going up or higher probability of going up in price than assets in a "bad" state. Importantly, assets could switch states with a certain probability, which generated short-run momentum similar to the forex market. The entire market structure, including the data-generating process, was conveyed to the investors at the start of the study. This allows us to compare performance relative to a normative benchmark strategy based on Bayesian updating which is directly linked to the propensity to hold losses versus gains. In this setting, a disposition effect above the normative benchmark translates directly to lower expected earnings. In turn, we take the measured disposition effect as an explicit proxy for performance.2

1See Heimer et al. (2020)for a discussion and demonstration on how the comparison between ex ante and ex post decisions canbeused to testforbehavioral biasesin the domain ofrisky choices.

Investors took part in one of four treatments that differed in whether investors o d s n ss s n cost for holding a losing position—as well as whether they had access to leverage. Consistent with our predictions, the introduction of opportunity costs substantially decreased the disposition effect and increased performance. While still higher than in the normative strategy, it was substantially smaller than when opportunity costs were minimized. However, even when investors could buy and sell in the same period, access to leverage increased the disposition effect to levels similar to those in the minimal opportunity cost condition. Consistent with our predictions, this increase was driven by a greater propensity to hold when traders' budget constraints are relaxed by leverage, their performance worsens because they can delay making the painful decision to realize losses.

The second experiment was designed to demonstrate the generality of our biases (e.g., the common stock traders in Odean 1998). In this experiment, tradersmadeaseries ofinvestmentdecisions over 3Operiods,either with or without access to leverage. Investors were endowed with 2,ooo units of experimental currency (5o0 units equals a $2 payout) and allocated these funds between six different risky assets. They were told that each asset had a probability of either going up or going down in price, and that this asset-specific probability remained constant throughout the experiment. Some assets had a higher probability of going up in price than others, but the exact probabilities were not revealed to the investors. In each period, the investor observed the new price of each asset and decided which to buy and which to sell. She could take as long as she wants to rebalance her portfolio subject to her budget constraint before continuing to the next period and making the same decision again. In the "No leverage" treatment, buying decisions were constrained by the portfolio's value and any outstanding balance in the cash account. In turn, holding on to an asset due to a reluctance to realize a loss came at the opportunity cost of not being able to pursue attractive alternatives. The Leverage" treatment offered more assets or leave deposited in their account. The borrowed amount was subtracted from their final earnings at the end of the experiment, which were then converted to U.S. dollars.

2 See also Frydman et al. (2014) and Frydman, Hartzmark, and Solomon (2018)for a similar approach tp comparing outcome-based trading strategies to a normative Bayesian benchmark as a measure of performance.

Results from the experiment replicate our findings from the first study and the forex market: Investors with access to leverage earned lower returns than those without it. Those with leverage earned nearly 150 fewer units of currency portfolio), and ended up with 200 fewer units of currency as their final wealth. Similar to the findings from the forex market and our first experiment, the negative relationship between leverage and performance was associated with an increased disposition effect.

This paper contributes to a growing literature at the intersection of behavioral economics and finance, specifically on how bounded rationality interacts with constraints on people's choices. Studies have examined how limited attention affects decisions when facing large choice sets (e.g., Huberman and Jiang 2006; Iyengar and Kamenica 2010; Goldreich and Hataburda 2013). Theories in this space suggest that constraints on cognitive capacity lead consumers to develop heuristic models of decision-making but are otherwise fully rational (e.g., Gabaix 2014). This may lead them to select simpler, less complicated options (Iyengar and Kamenica, 2010), make dominated choices (Bhargava, Loewenstein, and Sydnor, 2017), and sometimes prefer to avoid making a choice altogether (Iyengar and Lepper, 20o0). Cognitive constraints have been linked to the purchase of complex financial products that have poor returns (Carlin 2009; Celérier and Vallée 2017; Vokata 2021; Ghent, Torous, and Valkanov 2019; Gao et al. 2020). Our contribution differs from these findings in that we consider how expanding an individual's choice set can actually exacerbate existing systematic biases, such as the reluctance to realize losses. In this way, the paper is conceptually closest to the literature on commitment as a way of mitigating dynamic inconsistency in choices over time (O'Donoghue and Rabin, 1999). There, sophisticated individuals may choose to restrict their choice sets in order to avoid the temptation of choosing a smaller, sooner reward a larger benefit later).3 We contribute to this literature by demonstrating how restricting choice disciplines systematic biases in an economically important setting that has not been considered by prior work, namely, dynamic choice underuncertainty.

Our paper also has a more specific application to the role of financial leverage in individual decision-making. Research in this area is important because of the well-established connection between consumer leverage and aggregate outcomes (e.g., Geanakoplos 2010; Favara and Imbs 2015). The literature the most optimistic individuals. And both theoretical (e.g., Scheinkman and Xiong 2003, Simsek 2013) and empirical support corroborates this channel (e.g., Ben-David 2011, Koudijs and Voth 2016, Bailey, Davila, Kuchler, and Stroebel 2019, Braggion, Frehen, and Jerphanion 2020). We contribute to this literature by showing that financial leverage can also exacerbate behavioral biases attheindividuallevel.

3 While demand for commitment has been predicted theoreticall, this choice restriction has been rarely

documented in the"wild"(Laibson,2015).

Other research considers the effect of leverage or margin constraints on market characteristics, such as liquidity or asset prices (e.g., Kahraman and Tookes 2017, Jylha 2018, Boguth and Simutin 2018). Some specifically focus on the levered trading by retail investors (Hansman et al. 2018; Bian et al. 2021). Related to our paper, Heimer and Simsek (2019) find that changes in leverage constraints as a result of the CFTC regulation reduced traders' net losses at the monthly portfolio level and attribute 60% of the improvement to the reduction in transaction costs. Our paper examines the conceptually distinct question of whether constraints can discipline behavioral biases, and as a result, improve performance. Changes in leverage restrictions are considered as a specific case in which differences in constraints can be directly linked to biases and performance. Because we are interested in directly linking outcomes to decisions, we conduct tests at the trade-by-trade level after stripping leverage from the transaction. Our method is fundamentally different from the analyses of Heimer and Simsek (2019). Aside from the mechanical effect that leverage would have on net risk and return, there is no basis for leverage to affect trade-by-trade unlevered returns. In turn, we can examine how access to leverage affects performance through the channel of disciplining or exacerbating behavioral biases. Our experimental results further home in on the mechanism driving the observed effects.

Finally, the findings contribute to our understanding of the disposition effect by providing evidence on how constraints and opportunity costs play a crucial role in explaining the prevalence of this heavily studied bias. Recent work argues that investors use distinct mental accounts to construct "episodes" that are used to evaluate outcomes (Barberis and Xiong, 2012). Frydman, Hartzmark, and Solomon (2018) find that there is no disposition effect when traders reinvest their holdings in new positions. The authors conjecture that reinvesting losses into a similar position allows traders to "roll over" the associated mental account, avoiding closing it at a loss and deriving negative realization utility. We formally show that the option to "roll" a mental account acts to decrease the disposition effect through a complimentary but conceptually distinct mechanism from the presence of opportunity costs. While the former decreases the psychological costs of realizing a loss, the latter increases the economic costs of continuing to hold on to it. In both cases, however, leverage is predicted to increase the disposition effect, which our first experiment explicitly demonstrates.

1.Financial Constraints and Mistakes
------------------------------------

This section provides intuition on why leverage constraints would improve the outcomes of individuals' financial decision-making. We begin in the context of the realization utility framework developed in Barberis and Xiong (2012), in which people derive utility from realized gains and losses. In the model, individuals deviate from the normative framework in their relative propensity to hold losing assets for too long and to sell winning assets too soon (i.e., the disposition effect). This behavioral pattern leads people to make suboptimal market timing decisions under the simple assumption that there is some Jin (2013), the realization utility framework has been shown to offer realistic characterizations of individual investor behavior and its assumption on the relative disutility of realizing losses versus gains has been validated in several contexts, such as Frydman et al. (2014) and Hong, Loh, and Warachka (2021).

realistic extensions: investors have explicit budget constraints and there is an opportunity cost for investors to hold assets in their portfolio (other theories might use the term "liquidity needs"). Budget constraints have the effect of limiting the number of assets investors can hold at a time. In turn, when the opportunity cost of holding an asset is high—an attractive new investment opportunity becomes available or the investor has consumption needs— investors have a greater incentive to sell an asset they currently hold. Such opportunity costs increase the economic incentive to sell losses relative to the costs and realization utility increases the propensity to realize losses, while holding constant the propensity to realize gains, which mitigates individuals' disposition effect. This mechanism is similar in spirit to the one examined in Kelly (2018), where investors need to have particularly negative information

Having established that increasing opportunity costs reduce the propensity to hold losing assets, we then consider the effect of leverage. By allowing individuals to borrow to finance their purchases, leverage effectively relaxes individuals' budget constraints. Access to leverage reduces the costs of holding losses because traders can now take advantage of new opportunities without having to liquidate existing positions. Doing so lowers traders' willingness to realize losses closer to levels at which there is no opportunity cost for holding existing positions.4 Unlike the case of losses, realizing gains is attractive from a realization utility perspective. In turn, traders are motivated to realize gains with or without the presence of opportunity costs. Since the leverage effect operates by decreasing costs of holding on to an asset, its impact on the propensity to realize gains will be muted as well.5

4 Alternatively, if the initial asset was purchased using leverage, then subsequent purchases can be financed with traders'own capital, which would have the same effect on the propensity to realize losses.

Consequently, constraints on the provision of leverage can potentially improve outcomes by disciplining existing behavioral biases, namely, the propensity to delay realizing losses. The next section provides evidence for this prediction from transaction level data, showing that leverage constraints can indeed improve trading outcomes and decrease the disposition effect. We then conduct experiments that tightly links the theoretical predictions to our empirical tests.

2\. Evidence from Restricting Retail Traders' Leverage
------------------------------------------------------

2.1The retail forex market and CFTC regulations
-----------------------------------------------

The forex market has become an attractive venue for retail investors around the world. The size of the market was almost negligible in the early 2000s; by 2010, the forex market saw volume between $US 125 and $US 150 billion per day, which is roughly equivalent to the daily turnover on the entire NYSE family of stock exchanges (King and Rime 2010).

Retail forex brokerages act as a market-making system for retail clients. Clients use a domestic bank account to make deposits into their brokerage account. Brokerages do not provide currency exchange services, as customers cannot withdraw funds in a foreign currency. The brokerages have proprietary algorithms that are used to generate bid and ask quotes for their customers. These algorithms are based on each brokerage's inventory and a data feed from the interbank market. Quotes are offered to all clients in terms of the currency pair (e.g., EUR/USD) using the nomenclature designated by standard ISO 4217 from the International Standards Organization. The pair is expressed in terms of a "base" and "quote" currency. For example, in the EUR/USD pair, euros are the base and U.s. dollars are the quote. Traders decide how much of the pair to buy or to short in terms of the base currency.

Similar to other market-making systems, brokerages off-load assets into the interbank market and act as counterparties on all transactions. Brokerages generate revenue by charging a spread over interbank prices when offering bid and ask quotes to their clients, and brokerages do not charge additional fees per transaction. In turn, trading costs are a function of the transaction size and the bid-ask spread charged by the brokers (relative to the spreads in interbank markets). Retail brokerages offer the opportunity to use leverage at no additional costs. A client (U.S. or European) can, for example, take a 500,000 position in the EUR/USD using 50,0o0 of her own funds in domestic currency (U.S. dollars or euro). This client would then own a position that is levered 10:1.

5Traders mayhave other motives for not realizing a gain, such as option valueThe presence of opportunity costs still makes holding the winning position more expensive. This implies that access to leverage may increase the propensity to realize gains by lowering those costs. However, this increase is predicted to be lower than the propensity to realize losses, where the psychological motive points in the opposite direction of the opportunity costeffect.

for overseeing the U.S. retail forex market. The market was subject to few regulations in the 20o0s. Unlike mature markets where leverage is tightly regulated (e.g., at most 2:1 leverage is permitted on long positions of U.S. stocks), brokerages did not face external restrictions on the amount of leverage they could offer. Brokerages determined their own capital requirements and positions with over 100:1 leverage were regularly observed. Oversight of this market changed significantly when the Financial Crisis spurred concerns over the welfare of consumers participating in financial markets. The Dodd Frank included reforms targeting a wide array of financial industries. As part of the act, the CFTC's authority over the U.S. retail forex market was increased. In anticipation of Dodd-Frank's passage, the agency released a proposal on available to retail customers.6

The CFTC provided a rationale for the 10:1 leverage restriction it proposed. Specifically, the CFTC expressed concern that the amount of leverage available to retail forex traders exposed them to more downside risk than the agency was comfortable with. They were particularly worried about trader sophistication, noting that forex traders may be subject to counterparty risk which they might not be aware of. The proposed leverage restriction was an attempt to mitigate the traders' direct and indirect (counterparty) risks.' Given these concerns, the leverage restriction can be interpreted as a paternalistic policy intended to protect traders from excessive losses.

After the proposal was released, the CFTC received feedback from traders who mostly objected to the policy.8 The final policy document, which was released on September 10, 2010, outlined the set of rules for all retail brokerages thathadU.S.accounts.TheCFTCsummarizedinthedocumentthecomments it received and explained how, after careful consideration, the agency restricted the leverage of U.S. clients to 50:1 on all major currency pairs and 20:1 on all others (Table A.1 provides a complete list of currency pairs).9 All brokerages were required to comply with the regulations by October 18, 2010. Some began complying with the new leverage cap prior to the deadline. One high volume brokerage failed to comply with the regulation for several months after the date; it paid a heavy fine for this noncompliance. Meanwhile, such regulations were not under consideration in Europe. Domestic regulators continued to allow brokerages full discretion in the amount of leverage available to European clients. As a result, the maximum available leverage almost always exceeded 50:1 for E.U. traders.

6 [www.cftc.gov/LawRegulation/FederalRegister/ProposedRules/2010-456a](http://www.cftc.gov/LawRegulation/FederalRegister/ProposedRules/2010-456a)

7 Specifically, the document (p. 3291) states: "the Commission's proposed regulation regarding security deposits s intended both to mitigate the risk to which customers are exposed and to provide some capital to cover customer fundsheld by a failing firm."

8 The comments to the proposal can be found at the following link on the CFTC's website: [https://comments.cftc.gov/PublicComments/CommentList.aspx?id=748](https://comments.cftc.gov/PublicComments/CommentList.aspx?id=748)
.

9The final document can be found here: [http://www.cftc.gov/LawRegulation/FederalRegister/FinalRules/201021729](http://www.cftc.gov/LawRegulation/FederalRegister/FinalRules/201021729)
. It states: "the Commission's proposed leverage restriction was conservative and was proposed in an effort to provide maximum customer protection . . . Nevertheless, after considering the concerns expressed and arguments made in the comments,the Commission has determined to adopt a revised security deposit requirement

Our identification strategy relies on several unique features of the retail forex market. Forex brokerages often serve a global clientele. European and U.S. traders can be clients of the same brokerage but there is no overarching regulatory body overseeing these institutions; regulations are country specific and target only those with domestic bank accounts. Brokerages are responsible for complying with these regulations—for example, by making sure that U.S. traders cannot open positions with leverage exceeding 50:1—under threat of heavy fines and further legal actions. To open an account, a client is required to verify their country of origin by providing government-issued identification, such as a passport and a link to a domestic bank account. This structure is advantageous for research studying the effects of regulation because it is possible to compare restricted U.S. clients to their less restricted E.U. counterparts within the same brokerages. This controls for brokerage-specific institutional factors. Additionally, it is difficult and costly for traders to switch between control and treatment groups because doing so would require the retail client to obtain new foreign identification (e.g., a passport).

A related paper, Heimer and Simsek (2019), studies the effects of the CFTC regulations on the retail forex market. They find that the constraint had no effect on the market's liquidity or the bid-ask spreads charged by retail brokerages. Hence, their findings suggest that the CFTC leverage constraint is well-suited to identify the effectofleverage on trading behaviorbecause concurrent changes in theforex market are unlikely toconfound estimates of theleverage constraints' effect on trading performance and trading biases. While Heimer and Simsek (2019) study the regulation's effect on the supply-side of the market and on equilibrium market outcomes (volume and bid-ask spreads), they do not study the leverage constraint's effect on trading behavior.

2.2Trading data
---------------

Our analysis uses a data set, compiled by a social networking platform, that contains the individual transactions of retail forex traders.10 For privacy purposes, we refer to this platform as myForexBook. Registration with the platform is free. To register, the trader must be an active client at a retail-specific

10 There are few other data sets on retail forex traders. Ben-David, Birru, and Prokopenya 2018) and Heimer et al. (2020) use data from single brokerages. Heimer and Simon (2015) and Forman and Horton (2019) use data from

thesamesocial networkingplatform aswedo.
